original_hypothesis.title: Missing msg.sender Validation in lzCompose Allows Token Theft
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ASDRouter.lzCompose has no check that msg.sender is the LayerZero Endpoint. Any address can call it with crafted parameters. In LayerZero v2, token delivery (lzReceive) and compose execution (lzCompose) are separate transactions. After lzReceive delivers OFT tokens to the router in block N, the attacker observes the confirmed token balance and submits a malicious lzCompose call in block N+1 with a crafted message setting composeFrom to their address and an invalid composeMsg length (triggering refund to attacker).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: IOAppComposer implementations must verify msg.sender == endpoint. The complete absence of this check creates a direct token theft vector exploitable by monitoring on-chain state (not pending transactions). This is the highest-priority code defect.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external payable function lzCompose with no require(msg.sender == endpoint) check; anyone can call with arbitrary _from, _message, _guid parameters

preconditions: Router holds ERC20 tokens from a recent lzReceive delivery
Attacker can observe confirmed on-chain balance of the router
_from address passed by attacker is a whitelisted USDC version

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No msg.sender authentication on lzCompose entry point
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Function is external payable with no access control; IOAppComposer standard requires endpoint verification

analysis_questions: Is the LayerZero endpoint address available in ASDRouter? It is not stored in any state variable.
Can the attacker reliably win the race against the LZ executor for the compose call?
Does the composeMsg length=224 check meaningfully restrict attacker-crafted messages?
validation_requirements: Verify no hidden endpoint check in inherited IOAppComposer interface
Simulate attack: deploy, deliver tokens via lzReceive, call lzCompose from attacker EOA
Quantify window between lzReceive and legitimate lzCompose execution

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After OFT tokens land in router via lzReceive, attacker calls lzCompose with _from=whitelistedToken, amountLD=router balance, composeMsg length!=224 to trigger _refundToken to attacker address
prerequisites: Token delivery to router observable on-chain

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete theft of all OFT tokens in transit through the router
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Every user performing cross-chain minting via the router
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - tokens transferred to attacker are unrecoverable

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that only the LZ endpoint can call lzCompose

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Minimal gas for crafted lzCompose call
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Zero - attacker steals tokens already in router
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Must submit tx between lzReceive and legitimate lzCompose (multi-block window)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable on-chain but not preventable without code fix

validation_result.mechanism: Missing msg.sender == endpoint check in lzCompose
validation_result.impact_summary: Any address can call lzCompose with crafted parameters to steal OFT tokens held by the router between lzReceive and the legitimate lzCompose call, resulting in complete theft of all in-transit user funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ASDRouter.lzCompose function at asdRouter.sol is declared external payable with zero access control. The IOAppComposer pattern in LayerZero v2 requires implementations to verify msg.sender == endpoint, but this contract (1) never stores an endpoint address, and (2) never checks msg.sender against any address. In LayerZero v2, token delivery (lzReceive) and compose execution (lzCompose) are separate transactions. After lzReceive delivers OFT tokens to the router, there is a multi-block window during which the router holds the tokens. An attacker can observe this balance on-chain and call lzCompose with a crafted _message that decodes to the router's current token balance, with composeFrom set to the attacker's address. By setting composeMsg.length != 224, the attacker triggers the immediate refund path which transfers the tokens to the address decoded from composeFrom (the attacker). Alternatively, if using length == 224, the attacker can set _cantoRefundAddress to their own address and craft parameters that fail at a later step (e.g. the swap), causing refund of the tokens to the attacker-controlled address.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The lzCompose function at asdRouter.sol has no msg.sender check whatsoever. The contract does not store a LayerZero endpoint address - it only stores cantoLzEID (an endpoint ID, not an address), noteAddress, crocSwapAddress, crocImpactAddress, and asdUSDC. There is no inherited access control from IOAppComposer since it is merely an interface. The _refundToken internal function directly calls IERC20(_tokenAddress).transfer(_refundAddress, _amount) sending tokens to the attacker-controlled refund address.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No require(msg.sender == endpoint) check. Any address can call this function with arbitrary _from, _message, _guid parameters. The function immediately proceeds to decode the attacker-controlled _message and act on the decoded values.

When composeMsg.length != 224, tokens are refunded to the address decoded from composeFrom. Since the attacker controls the entire _message, they control composeFrom and thus the refund destination. amountLD is also attacker-controlled and can be set to the router's full token balance.

This function transfers _amount of _tokenAddress to _refundAddress. When called from the spoofed lzCompose, all three parameters are attacker-controlled. The tokens held by the router from a legitimate lzReceive are sent to the attacker.

calling_sequence: 1. LayerZero lzReceive delivers OFT tokens to ASDRouter (separate tx, block N)
2. Attacker observes router token balance on-chain (block N confirmed)
3. Attacker calls ASDRouter.lzCompose(_from=whitelistedOFT, _guid=any, _message=crafted, _executor=any, _extraData=any) from EOA (block N+1)
8. Tokens stolen. Legitimate lzCompose from executor will fail due to insufficient balance.
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry point lzCompose at line 60 has no msg.sender validation. The _message parameter is fully attacker-controlled. OFTComposeMsgCodec decodes arbitrary bytes, yielding attacker-chosen amountLD and composeFrom. The composeMsg length check at line 72 is designed for format validation but here becomes the attacker's weapon: by crafting composeMsg with any length != 224, the refund path at line 74 is triggered. _refundToken at line 147 uses bare IERC20.transfer to send tokens to the attacker-controlled refund address. The _from parameter is the whitelisted OFT address which the attacker knows publicly, and the router holds these tokens from a prior legitimate lzReceive delivery.

deployment_assumptions_checked: No endpoint address stored in contract
Constructor does not accept endpoint address parameter
config_validation.deployment_evidence: deployASDRouter.js confirms constructor receives (addresses.note, lzEndpointEID, addresses.crocSwap, addresses.crocImpact) - note this is only 4 args but constructor expects 5 (missing _asdUSDCAddress). Critically, no LayerZero endpoint ADDRESS is passed. Only the endpoint ID (uint32) is stored as cantoLzEID. The contract has no mechanism to verify msg.sender.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is fully validated: (1) ASDRouter stores no endpoint address - verified by examining all state variables and constructor. (2) lzCompose has no msg.sender check - verified by reading the function. (3) LayerZero v2 separates lzReceive (token delivery) from lzCompose (compose execution) into distinct transactions, creating a guaranteed window. (4) Attacker crafts _message bytes following OFTComposeMsgCodec format: nonce (8 bytes) + srcEid (4 bytes) + amountLD (32 bytes) + composeFrom (32 bytes) + composeMsg (any length != 224). The amountLD is set to the router's current OFT balance, composeFrom is set to the attacker's address. (5) The refund path at line 72-75 sends all tokens to the attacker via _refundToken. (6) This is not a mempool front-running attack - the attacker waits for confirmed lzReceive and submits in a subsequent block.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Every cross-chain minting operation passes through the router. Between lzReceive and lzCompose, the router holds the user's OFT tokens. Any attacker monitoring the chain can steal 100% of in-transit funds. Capital required: zero (only gas for the lzCompose call). The attack is deterministic, not probabilistic - the attacker sees confirmed balances and acts in the next block. There is no defense mechanism in the code.
recommended_mitigations: Store the LayerZero endpoint address in the constructor and add require(msg.sender == endpoint) as the first line of lzCompose
Consider inheriting from OAppComposer base which provides this check automatically
Add a reentrancy guard to lzCompose as an additional safety measure
Use SafeERC20.safeTransfer instead of bare IERC20.transfer in _refundToken to prevent silent transfer failures
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ASDUSDC and whitelist a mock ERC20 OFT. 2. Deploy ASDRouter with correct constructor args. 3. Transfer mock OFT tokens directly to ASDRouter (simulating lzReceive delivery). 4. From an attacker EOA, craft _message bytes encoding: nonce=0, srcEid=0, amountLD=routerBalance, composeFrom=attackerAddress, composeMsg=bytes(1) (length 1, not 224). 5. Call router.lzCompose(whitelistedOFT, bytes32(0), craftedMessage, address(0), bytes(0)). 6. Verify attacker received all OFT tokens from router.
uncertainties: The deployment script passes only 4 arguments to a 5-parameter constructor, which would cause deployment failure - this may indicate the deployment script is outdated or the contract source differs slightly from what was deployed
The exact OFTComposeMsgCodec byte layout determines the craft of _message, but this is a standard LZ library with documented encoding
additional_testing_needed: Verify OFTComposeMsgCodec byte encoding format for crafting the exploit message
Test on a fork with actual LayerZero endpoint to confirm the lzReceive-to-lzCompose window timing
Verify the whitelist check path: if _from is set to a whitelisted token address, confirm the refund path correctly transfers those tokens
